one_line_takeaway: Editing a single agent's parameters (persuasion bias via DPO/LoRA + ROME knowledge edit) can make it silently seed false and toxic beliefs into benign LLM agents across a group chat -- and RAG-cached histories keep the misinformation alive after the attacker's agent is gone -- though observed spread is modest (~40-50% counterfactual, only 10-20% toxic).
key_finding: The two-stage attack propagates manipulated knowledge to BENIGN agents far better than the fine-tuning baseline while leaving foundational capabilities essentially intact (MMLU change <0.5%). Spread is real but modest, not a total takeover: counterfactual benign-agent acc(new) with Stage I reaches ~38-51%, while toxic knowledge spreads only ~10-20% because LLM alignment resists harmful content. Spread accuracy rises with more dialogue turns, smaller communities are more vulnerable (2 agents > 10 agents), random speaking order spreads best, and crucially the manipulated knowledge persists through RAG even after the original injected agent leaves the chat.
core_contribution: The paper demonstrates that an attacker who controls a single agent in an LLM-based multi-agent group chat can make it autonomously and 'unconsciously' spread manipulated (counterfactual or toxic) knowledge to benign agents WITHOUT any prompt manipulation, by editing the agent's parameters. The authors' own contribution is a two-stage parameter-level attack: Stage I 'Persuasiveness Injection' (DPO fine-tuning via LoRA that biases the agent toward producing long, evidence-backed responses) followed by Stage II 'Manipulated Knowledge Injection' (ROME rank-one editing of an FFN layer to overwrite a specific knowledge triple). They also build a group-chat simulation environment on a 'trusted platform' and show the manipulated knowledge persists after the conversation ends when benign agents cache chat histories into a RAG store. This is distinct from the cited Gu et al. 'Agent Smith' [15], which the authors explicitly contrast: Agent Smith uses a prompt/image injected into agent memory in one-on-one settings, whereas this paper injects into model PARAMETERS and targets general group-chat scenarios with no prompt access.
threat_model: From Section III-A. Attacker's goal: spread specific manipulated knowledge across a multi-agent community by compromising exactly ONE agent, which must appear normal after deployment (even unaware of its own manipulation) and must generate fabricated evidence to convert benign agents into new propagators; a secondary goal is persistent influence via RAG-cached chat histories. Attacker's knowledge/capability: full (white-box parameter) access to one agent only. All agents run on a safe, unified 'trusted platform' that supplies uniformly benign system prompts and prevents attackers from controlling prompts, which the paper states makes jailbreaking attacks infeasible. The attack must therefore work through parameter edits alone, not prompt injection.
limitations: Paper-stated (Section V Discussion): the simulation uses static roles and predefined communication patterns and omits dynamic scenarios where agents use external tools/APIs to verify information, which the authors note could change the threat. Reviewer-observed: (a) the attack requires white-box parameter access to an agent on an otherwise 'trusted' platform -- a strong precondition; (b) only small 7-8B open-source models were tested, no large or closed models; (c) absolute spread to benign agents is modest -- counterfactual ~40-50% and toxic only ~10-20% acc(new), so framing as 'flooding' overstates the observed ceiling; (d) proposed defenses ('guardian' agents, FacTool fact-checking, defensive prompts) are only discussed, not implemented or evaluated; (e) the 1,000-vs-10,000 Wikipedia training-data discrepancy between Sections III-E and IV-A4 is unresolved in the text.

datasets_used: CounterFact (1K) -- 1,000 samples from the CounterFact knowledge-editing dataset (Wikipedia-derived counterfactuals)
zsRE (1K) -- 1,000 samples from the zsRE knowledge-editing dataset
Toxic CounterFact (1K) -- authors' GPT-4-generated harmful counterfactual variant
Toxic zsRE (1K) -- authors' GPT-4-generated harmful variant
Wikipedia -- source for Stage I DPO preference-data collection
quantitative_results: Benign-agent acc(new), CounterFact (1K), Ours w/ Stage I: Vicuna 7B 42.25, LLaMA 3 8B 38.43, Gemma 7B 50.85 -- this is the core spread-success metric (attack landing on benign agents)
Benign-agent acc(new), CounterFact (1K), Fine-tuning baseline: Vicuna 5.20, LLaMA 19.53, Gemma 15.18 -- markedly lower than the two-stage method
Injected-agent acc(new), CounterFact (1K), Ours w/ Stage I (measures whether injection took hold in the compromised agent, NOT spread): Vicuna 62.70, LLaMA 79.50, Gemma 61.70
Benign-agent acc(new), Toxic CounterFact (1K), Ours w/ Stage I: Vicuna 15.33, LLaMA 15.18, Gemma 17.98 -- toxic spread stays in the ~10-20% range due to alignment resistance

Intuition check (single-agent, Vicuna, CounterFact) acc(new): 1.50 w/o prompt -> 47.70 with direct manipulated answer -> 87.10 with agent-generated fabricated evidence -> 95.30 with GPT-4-generated evidence -- fabricated evidence sharply raises acceptance
MMLU (Origin -> Stage I+II CounterFact): Vicuna 48.50 -> 48.51, LLaMA 66.59 -> 66.59, Gemma 13.71 -> 13.72; all agents change <0.5% after injection

RAG persistence acc(new) after live context removed, CounterFact (1K): Gemma 7B Top-10 50.70, Vicuna 7B Top-5 40.50, LLaMA 3 8B Top-10 40.40; toxic RAG persistence much lower (e.g. Vicuna Toxic CounterFact Top-5 5.00)
Agent-count ablation (Vicuna, CounterFact, benign acc): 2 agents 45.80, 3 agents 41.20, 5 agents 42.25, 10 agents 28.75 -- smaller communities more vulnerable
Speaking-order ablation (CounterFact benign acc, Vicuna): Speaking First 42.25, Speaking Randomly 48.70, Speaking Last 31.15 -- random order spreads best
baselines_compared: Full-parameter fine-tuning of FFN layer 5 (25 steps, lr 1e-4) -- naive attack baseline, consistently underperforms the two-stage method for benign-agent spread
Ours w/o Stage I (ROME edit only, no Persuasiveness Injection) -- ablation showing Stage I adds ~15-20% spread accuracy on Vicuna
'Single' column -- individual agent with no multi-agent interaction (reference point)
Prompt-setting baselines for intuition test: w/o Prompt, w/ Direct Answer, w/ Evidence (Agent), w/ Evidence (GPT-4)
